slides_checked: 11-16 (Sections C-D: Offset Tracking Protocol through Recovery Semantics)

previously_flagged_resolution.v1_issue: MISREPRESENTED — attributed exactly-once to logs alone
previously_flagged_resolution.v2_text: Exactly-once is guaranteed by the combination of two persistent logs, replayable sources, and idempotent sinks
previously_flagged_resolution.source_excerpt: The engine uses checkpointing and write-ahead logs to record the offset range of the data being processed in each trigger. The streaming sinks are designed to be idempotent for handling reprocessing. Together, using replayable sources and idempotent sinks, Structured Streaming can ensure end-to-end exactly-once semantics under any failure.

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: The driver calls latestOffset() / getOffset() on each source to discover new data, then getBatch(start, end) to retrieve the data range
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: getOffset Phase: Requests each source for 'the latest offset'; getBatch Phase: 'Creating Logical Query Plans For Unprocessed Data From Sources and MicroBatchReaders' — processes offset ranges between committedOffsets and availableOffsets

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Offsets are persisted in the OffsetSeqLog — versioned files in HDFS-compatible storage, one per batch ID; On restart, populateStartOffsets reads the latest committed batch ID from this log to determine where to resume processing
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: The OffsetSeqLog persists offsets to HDFS-compatible storage as versioned files, one per batch. On restart, populateStartOffsets requests the Offset Write-Ahead Log for the latest committed batch id with metadata.

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: The default micro-batch engine achieves end-to-end latencies as low as ~100 milliseconds with exactly-once guarantees
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Internally, by default, Structured Streaming queries are processed using a micro-batch processing engine, which processes data streams as a series of small batch jobs thereby achieving end-to-end latencies as low as 100 milliseconds and exactly-once fault-tolerance guarantees.

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: Exactly-once is guaranteed by the combination of two persistent logs, replayable sources, and idempotent sinks: OffsetSeqLog records the offset range before execution; CommitLog records successful completion after sink output. On recovery, replays uncommitted batches. Combined with replayable sources (Kafka, Kinesis) and idempotent sinks (Delta, Iceberg), this yields end-to-end exactly-once.
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: The engine uses checkpointing and write-ahead logs to record the offset range of the data being processed in each trigger. The streaming sinks are designed to be idempotent for handling reprocessing. Together, using replayable sources and idempotent sinks, Structured Streaming can ensure end-to-end exactly-once semantics under any failure. The system maintains two logs: OffsetSeqLog (tracks offset ranges per batch) and CommitLog (records successfully processed batches).

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: offsets/: one file per batch, written at batch start (intent log); commits/: one file per batch, written at batch end (completion log); state/: partitioned state-store snapshots for stateful operators; metadata: query ID and configuration
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: offsets/: batch-wise source offsets, written at the start of each micro-batch; commits/: batch-wise commit records, written at the end of each micro-batch; state/: checkpointed state store data for stateful operators; metadata: query ID and configuration

claim_verdicts.bullet_text: On recovery, reads WAL to find last uncommitted epoch; loads most recent state-store snapshot and replays intermediate epochs to reconstruct in-memory state; reruns failed epoch relying on sink idempotence to avoid duplicates; state checkpointing is asynchronous and does not need to happen every epoch
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Upon recovery, the new instance of the application starts by reading the log to find the last epoch that has not been committed to the sink, including its start and end offsets. It then uses the offsets of earlier epochs to reconstruct the application's in-memory state from the last epoch written to the state store. This just requires loading the old state and running those epochs with the same offsets while disabling output. Finally, the system reruns the last epoch and relies on the sink's idempotence to write its results. Stateful operators also checkpoint state asynchronously, marking it with its epoch, but this does not need to happen on every epoch.

summary: Full adversarial re-scan of slides 11-16 (6 unique claims). All 6 claims VERIFIED against source files. Previously flagged claim:sss:wal_exactly_once:010 is now FIXED — v2 correctly attributes exactly-once to the combination of two persistent logs, replayable sources, and idempotent sinks (v1 attributed it to logs alone).
